title: MSIX/Store App Failure During ESP (AppX Deployment)
description: Detects when the Enrollment Status Page failed on the Apps subcategory and the agent's AppX deployment log scan identified a suspected MSIX/Store package. Store/MSIX apps install outside the Win32 pipeline, so the regular per-app tracking sees nothing — this correlation names the likely culprit.

preconditions.description: Scope this rule to sessions where the settle-window AppX scan found a package whose deployment error matches the ESP failure HRESULT. Weaker verdicts (appx_activity_no_hresult_match, no_appx_candidates) intentionally do not fire this rule — they carry too little signal for an admin-facing finding.

explanation:
The ESP **{{espCategory}}/Apps** subcategory failed with HRESULT **{{espErrorCode}}** — an error from the AppX/MSIX deployment stack, i.e. a **Microsoft Store / MSIX app**, not a Win32 app. These apps install via the EnterpriseModernAppManagement CSP and never appear in the IME Win32 sidecar logs, which is why all tracked Win32/WinGet apps in this session can look green while ESP still reports an Apps failure.

The agent scanned the AppXDeploymentServer event log during the failure settle window and identified **{{topCandidatePackageName}}** (package `{{topCandidatePackage}}`) as the most likely culprit — its deployment error matches the ESP failure code ({{candidateCount}} candidate(s) total, confidence: {{confidence}}).

**This is a correlation-based assessment, not a confirmed root cause** — validate against the AppXDeploymentServer/Operational event log on the device or in the diagnostics archive before acting.

remediation.title: Confirm the failing package
steps: Open Event Viewer → Applications and Services Logs → Microsoft → Windows → AppXDeploymentServer → Operational on the device (or in the diagnostics archive if uploaded)
Filter for Error-level events around the ESP failure time and confirm the package name and HRESULT match this finding
Look up the HRESULT: 0x80073cf9 = generic install failure, 0x80073cf0 = invalid/corrupt package, 0x80073cff = sideloading/licensing policy block, 0x80073d02 = blocked because app is in use

remediation.title: Check Store app assignment and licensing
steps: Intune portal → Apps → check the assignment of the suspected app: device context vs user context — user-context Store apps installing during Account Setup ESP are a common failure source
Verify the app's license type (online vs offline): online-licensed Store apps require the user's Entra ID token and Store connectivity during ESP
Check whether the Microsoft Store endpoints are reachable from the enrollment network (proxy/firewall allowlists)
If the app is not business-critical during provisioning: remove it from the ESP blocking-app list so a failure no longer terminates enrollment

remediation.title: Consider repackaging
steps: For maximum ESP reliability, wrap the app as a Win32 (.intunewin) deployment instead of a Store/MSIX assignment — Win32 installs are fully tracked and produce per-app failure telemetry
Alternatively deploy the app post-enrollment (available, not required) so it cannot block the ESP
